docs.search_text: Constitution Scorecard Use this document to track the quality of the draft over time. The goal is not just to rate the text, but to identify where improvement work should go next. Scoring Scale Score each area from 1 to 10 . 10 — Excellent; coherent, enforceable, and near final 8–9 — Strong; only targeted revisions needed 6–7 — Good foundation; meaningful revision still needed 4–5 — Conceptually promising but underdeveloped or unstable 1–3 — Major redesign needed Evaluation Criteria Score each section on these dimensions: | Criterion | What to Ask | | | | | Clarity | Is the rule understandable on first read? | | Internal Consistency | Does it fit cleanly with the rest of the Constitution? | | Enforceability | Can a court, commission, or political actor actually apply it? | | Democratic Legitimacy | Does it preserve accountability and majority rule with minority protection? | | Abuse Resistance | Does it close obvious bad faith or authoritarian loopholes? | | Draft Discipline | Is it concise enough to function as constitutional text rather than statutory code? | Suggested scoring method: 1. Give each criterion a score from 1 to 10. 2. Average them for a section score. 3. Round to the nearest half point if needed. 4. Record the main reason for the score and the next fix to improve it. Current Baseline These are the current working scores for the draft after the latest simplification and consistency revisions. Summary Baseline | Area | Score | Status | Main Strength | Main Weakness | Next Improvement Target | | | | | | | | | Preamble | 9.0 | Strong | Clear statement of purpose and democratic intent in durable founding prose with anti capture language now anchored more directly to law and equal citizenship | Remaining work is limited to cadence and memorability rather than substance or exploit risk | Final polish for rhythm and memorability | | Article I — Electoral System | 9.1 | Strong | Modern, coherent democratic architecture now validated not only against formal ballot exclusion but also against softer exclusion from core public or publicly regulated election infrastructure through new §§9.5 and 10.6 | Administration details remain a bit dense, and quasi private cartelization outside the new constitutional floor still needs scenario pressure | Simplify without losing safeguards and add one more softer cartel scenario family | | Article II — Legislature | 9.0 | Strong | Mandatory vote enforcement, continuity rules, and recess evasion bar all verified by simulation; later discipline passes also compressed committee discharge, floor vote timing, and anti delay mechanics into cleaner constitutional standards while preserving the anti obstruction structure | Draft discipline has improved, but §15A.4 coordinated absenteeism and a few continuity provisions remain denser than ideal even though they are simulation validated and likely necessary | Continue compressing any remaining procedural detail that can safely move to statute | | Article III — Executive | 9.0 | Strong | Emergency lapse, impeachment fallback, anti evasion rules, and recess evasion enforcement all verified; §10.2A now has a 7 day ACC certification deadline with House member backup path; high impact presidential directives now point cleanly to a dedicated fast track review path in Article IV | Emergency review remains the most recurring practical bottleneck — the text is correct but political nonperformance is the residual risk | Keep stress testing re declaration and lapse certificate refused edge cases | | Article IV — Judiciary | 9.0 | Strong | The recent judicial hardening provisions are now directly validated in simulation, and the article now provides a narrow fast track for high impact presidential directives before ordinary delay can harden into fait accompli governance; later discipline passes also compressed pool diversity, oversight, recusal, and conduct certification language without changing the enforcement structure | Strategic delay remains an intentional stress case, and broader judicial governance edge cases still deserve later review | Extend later scenario coverage into broader judicial governance and delay edge cases | | Article V — Civil and Political Rights | 8.9 | Strong | Broad, modern rights coverage now has direct simulation support for political speech, equal protection, overbroad rights suspension, data broker warrant evasion, school religion neutrality, bodily autonomy, family autonomy, and government internet access punishment | The article is now broadly validated; remaining work is more about later compression and optional refinement than missing coverage | Treat further Article V scenario building as optional refinement unless a new exploit appears | | Article VI — Democratic Integrity | 9.2 | Near final | Excellent anti subversion framing and election defense focus, now with direct resilience coverage for covert foreign shell routing, dominant civic intermediary continuity, and rapid corrective relief against manipulative visibility of official emergency and election information | Some media and enforcement provisions may still need narrowing at the boundary with protected speech, and Article VI §7.5A should stay tightly limited to indispensable civic intermediaries | Stress test media pluralism and information integrity speech boundaries and watch §7.5A for overbreadth | | Article VII — Campaign Finance and Political Money | 8.8 | Strong | Sustained compression passes have brought the article to genuine constitutional principle level: traceable disclosure, foreign money prohibition, public financing mandate, outside spending limits, and enforcement all stated as constitutional rules without statutory machinery, and the latest pass further trimmed adequacy review and election window mechanics | Remaining density is now concentrated mostly in §2.2's parameter enumeration rather than across the article as a whole | Final polish pass on §2.2 if a later review surfaces residual density | | Article VIII — Government Ethics | 9.2 | Near final | Strong anti corruption architecture now states more clearly that Article VIII owns substantive ethics and market integrity duties while Article XIII owns general agency structure, Article 1.13 now reaches concealed foreign and criminal beneficial ownership in major federal contracting, and recent compression has stripped away more of the registry style mechanics without weakening enforcement | The contractor transparency clause still needs a final abstraction level check, but the remaining issue is narrower than before | Keep Article 1.13 under watch during the final whole draft discipline pass | | Article IX — Citizenship and National Membership | 8.7 | Strong | Equal citizenship, overseas political membership, officeholding equality, sensitive office narrowing, and fair proof rules now all have direct scenario support, and §5.2 loss of citizenship now requires notice, independent tribunal hearing, right to counsel, and judicial review before revocation takes effect | Only a small number of edge cases remain, mostly at the boundary between nationality based office safeguards and ordinary civic equality | Add one later scenario on renunciation, statelessness avoidance, or foreign office incompatibility, then stop unless a new exploit appears | | Article X — Federalism and the States | 8.8 | Strong | Modernized federalism now has direct validation for democratic floor enforcement, anti commandeering, implied preemption limits, local self government, and the territorial limits on state regulation of digital conduct | The remaining work is now mostly optional edge case expansion and later prose simplification rather than a validation deficit | Treat further Article X scenario building as optional refinement unless a new exploit appears | | Article XI — Amendments | 8.8 | Strong | The article now has clearer anti entrenchment doctrine and direct simulation coverage: evasive authoritarian amendments are rejected cleanly, and pre clearance delay no longer stalls the amendment path indefinitely; discipline passes removed embedded rationale text, explanatory commentary, and some standing detail clutter without changing the core amendment tracks | Pre clearance still adds a real judicial delay bottleneck in rights amendment timeout scenarios, so the review layer must remain narrow and disciplined | Keep the current structure unless new scenarios show over judicialization or an under specified ratification edge case | | Article XII — Constitutional Organs | 9.0 | Near final | Duplicate vacancy sections merged into a single shared §5.6; the two Organs now share one clean vacancy and temporary appointment rule rather than two identical paragraphs; §5.5 internal anti sabotage is directly validated; and the Article XIX bridge mechanism is now validated through actual startup operations under a temporary quorum | The remaining issue is no longer whether the continuity architecture works, but whether later whole draft compression can keep the article this clear without losing the validated protections | Treat further Article XII scenario building as optional refinement unless a broader continuity exploit appears | | Article XIII — Federal Agencies | 8.8 | Strong | Strong anti capture architecture now includes an explicit constitutional anchor for continuity and integrity standards in critical civilian infrastructure, and recent compression has further reduced code like reporting and revolving door administration detail | A few agency governance rules could still be compressed, especially where continuity standards may drift toward operational detail | Continue pruning operational detail while preserving the new critical infrastructure anchor | | Article XIV — Taxation and Public Revenue | 8.6 | Strong | Taxation now clearly owns the constitutional rules of public revenue, protects simple or automatic tax administration, and now has direct scenario support for two core edge cases: unilateral executive continuation of temporary emergency revenue measures and selective customs duty favoritism without statutory authority | The main remaining question is whether any later scenario exposes a harder boundary around complex revenue preferences or tax administration abuse, not whether the basic constitutional floor is real | Treat further Article XIV scenario building as optional refinement unless a more exotic revenue exploit appears | | Article XV — Budget, Public Credit, and Appropriations | 8.8 | Strong | Budget continuity, debt, fiscal analysis, and classified spending safeguards now have a much clearer and more authoritative constitutional home | A final compression pass may still be needed to ensure the stronger safeguards do not drift back toward code like detail | Run one simplification pass after simulation and verify no residual fiscal duplication remains | | Article XVI — War Powers and National Security | 8.8 | Strong | The war powers enforcement chain is now much better validated: nonauthorization, withdrawal order, fiscal hold, Treasury implementation, and automatic impeachment transmission all execute coherently under stress | It is still the hardest defiance scenario in the suite because the main remaining bottlenecks are congressional and House nonperformance at the worst point in the chain | Add deeper command chain and nuclear refusal stress tests, then consider whether any further missed vote default rule is warranted | | Article XVII — Foreign Policy and National Security | 8.5 | Strong | Non war national security framework is clearer and better bounded | Gray zone and intelligence provisions still need edge case testing | Stress test executive stretch scenarios | | Article XVIII — Social, Economic, and Affirmative Rights | 8.7 | Strong | All four floor enforcement scenarios now validated: climate backsliding, education floor inequality, housing floor failure, and child welfare representation defect all produce clear judicial outcomes without courts prescribing policy; anti backsliding rule and judicial declaration without prescription boundary both confirmed | Courts' post declaration compliance monitoring in climate and education cases still untested under hostile political conditions | Extend scenario coverage to government non compliance with remedial plan order | | Article XIX — Ratification and Transition | 9.0 | Near final | First cycle election and organ setup scenarios validate that transition deadlines convert into continuity preserving fallbacks, the bridge mechanism now shows real startup operations under temporary quorum rather than a merely formal judicial appointment power, §6.5 directly protects federal records continuity during transition, and recent compression reduced implementation code like bridge and corrective legislation detail | The remaining stress point is political nonperformance before the bridge activates, not uncertainty about what happens afterward | Keep targeted first cycle testing optional unless a broader administrative sabotage exploit appears | | Cross References and Draft Integration | 9.0 | Strong | The draft now coordinates legislative continuity, emergency lapse, judicial fallback, transition fallback, and simulation coverage cleanly across the reordered architecture | Historical notes and residual drafting artifacts still deserve one final cleanup pass, and the remaining bottlenecks are now concentrated in a few deadline driven chains rather than broad structural drift | Review the remaining deadline driven chains, then do a final whole draft consistency pass | | Overall Draft | 9.0 | Strong | The architecture is now materially cleaner and the simulator still shows a closure oriented system with 0 unresolved obligations even under a larger, harsher suite; recent validation and compression passes have shifted the project firmly into final refinement territory | The remaining weakness is no longer missing machinery; it is a mix of deadline driven political nonperformance, a few judicial and institutional delay chains, and residual code like density in a handful of articles | Focus on final consistency, selective compression, and publication rather than new constitutional machinery | Baseline This is a comparative baseline for the current United States Constitution using the same rubric. These scores are necessarily more synthetic because the present Constitution relies heavily on later amendments, statutes, institutions, and judicial doctrine to function. | Area | Score | Status | Main Strength | Main Weakness | Comparative Note | | | | | | | | | Current US Constitution | 6.0 | Mixed | Durable framework with strong civil liberty traditions and stable transfer of power norms under good faith conditions | Major democratic distortions, weak anti authoritarian safeguards, and heavy dependence on extra constitutional patchwork | Strong historical legitimacy, weak fit for current democratic and governance failures | | Criterion | Score | Comparative Reason | | | | | | Clarity | 7.0 | Concise and canonical, but many core rules are under specified for modern governance | | Internal Consistency | 6.0 | Amended over time without full redesign, leaving tensions between original structure and later guarantees | | Enforceability | 6.5 | Courts can enforce many limits, but some core structural failures are politically entrenched rather than judicially remediable | | Democratic Legitimacy | 4.5 | Electoral College, malapportioned Senate, and extreme amendment difficulty permit durable minority rule | | Abuse Resistance | 4.5 | The design assumes good faith and leaves dangerous openings around coups, corruption, emergency power, and partisan hardball | | Draft This confirms that §5.5 is operational rather than merely aspirational | The remaining live issue for Article XII is not internal sabotage but startup delay and continuity interaction with Article XIX | | 2026 03 29 | Article VIII / Article XIII | 9.1 / 8.7 | 9.1 / 8.8 | Boundary review completed: Article VIII now states more explicitly that it owns substantive ethics and market integrity duties while Article XIII governs general agency structure, Article XIII now recognizes article specific regulators as a distinct subcategory, and the stale cross reference to nonexistent Article VIII §2.10 was removed | Remaining work is light compression, especially Article VIII §1.13 and some operational detail in Article XIII | | 2026 03 29 | Article VIII | 9.1 | 9.2 | Organized crime and financial capture work now validates both the no too big to prosecute rule and the broadened contractor beneficial ownership clause, which now reaches concealed domestic criminal enterprise control of major federal contractors directly | Article VIII Section 1.13 is now stronger but also more likely to need a later compression pass so it stays at the right constitutional level | | 2026 03 29 | Article VIII / Article XIII / Article XIX | 9.2 / 8.8 / 8.9 | 9.2 / 8.8 / 8.9 | Light compression pass removed some of the most implementation code like detail from contractor disclosure reporting, revolving door transition support/reporting, and transition bridge/corrective legislation mechanics without changing the validated continuity or anti capture structure | These are discipline gains rather than score driving structural changes; Both now produce clean judicial relief, which materially strengthens the article's validation depth beyond speech, equal protection, suspension, and data purchase scenarios | One or two later family autonomy or digital rights scenarios would finish the article's main validation arc | | 2026 03 29 | Article IX — Citizenship and National Membership | 8.6 | 8.7 | Added direct scenario coverage for the two remaining obvious validation gaps: overbroad sensitive office nationality restrictions and time sensitive citizenship proof disputes. delay edge cases beyond the currently validated provisions | | 2026 03 29 | Article IV / Article V | 8.8 / 8.5 | 8.9 / 8.7 | New simulation coverage directly validated Article IV's shadow docket / Article XIII | 9.0 / 8.5 | 9.1 / 8.6 | Narrow compression pass removed code like sanctions and registry mechanics from Article VIII's financial integrity rules and reduced Article XIII's political appointee cap to a cleaner constitutional floor plus anti evasion and public accounting rules | A few agency governance and ethics administration clauses can still be pruned in a later whole draft compression pass | | 2026 03 29 | Article XIV — Taxation and Public Revenue | 8.3 | 8.4 | Added a narrow administrative fairness clause allowing simplified or automatic assessment, withholding, and collection while preserving due process, error correction, and relief from penalties caused principally by state created complexity or reporting failure | Emergency revenue powers and edge case revenue authorities still need pressure testing | | 2026 03 29 | Article IV — Judiciary | 8.5 | 8.8 | Four structural gaps closed: §9.7 shadow docket transparency (emergency orders require written explanation within 48h, voidable without one); §7.3 recusal enforcement (refusal triggers automatic Conduct Board referral + voidable judgment); §8.1 Conduct Board certification deadlock (60 day deadline + 2/3 chamber override backstop); §10.4 lower court expedited delay (mandatory explanation, reassignment, neglect referral); §4.9/4.10 consolidated; §9.2A Clerk registry language removed | Strategic delay bottlenecks in simulation are designed stress point failures; no further structural gaps identified | | 2026 03 29 | Article VII | 8.6 | 8.8 | Second compression pass removed §1.3 remedies paragraph, §1.4 real time reporting machinery, §1.6 implementation paragraph, §5.1 statutory mandate sentence, §5.3 enumerated list, and §6.2 remedies enumeration; draft discipline 7.8 → 8.5 | Minor residual density in §2.2 parameter enumeration and §3.1 election window definition; not worth another pass until a later global review | | 2026 03 29 | Article III / Art XIX / Art VII / Art VIII | 9.0 / 8.8 / 8.5 / 9.0 | 9.0 / 8.9 / 8.6 / 9.0 | Art III §8 scaffolding replaced with clean cross reference; §10.2A gained 7 day ACC certification deadline with House member backup path; Art XIX §7.1 corrective legislation power narrowed to three enumerated defect types, closing shadow amendment power risk; Art VII §1.2, §1.5, §4.2 compressed to constitutional principle level; Art VIII §§2.7–2.9 consolidated and §2.10 revolving door collapsed from 8 sub subsections to one section | Art VII still has statutory grade detail in §1.3, §1.6, §3.2 — another compression pass is warranted | | 2026 03 29 | Article XVIII — Social, Economic, and Affirmative Rights | 8.0 | 8.4 | The article was hardened by clarifying constitutional floors, strengthening anti backsliding protection, and removing some of the most code like wage and child procedure detail | It still lacks direct simulation coverage, so the next step is scenario validation rather than more doctrinal expansion | | 2026 03 29 | Article XI — Amendments | 8.6 | 8.8 | The amendment article was strengthened and then directly exercised in simulation: evasive authoritarian redesign is now rejected cleanly at pre clearance, while judicial inaction in a rights amendment case no longer blocks the process because clearance is deemed granted on timeout | The remaining live risk is over judicialization or repeated pre clearance delay, not missing constitutional machinery | | 2026 03 29 | Article XII — Constitutional Organs | 8.0 | 8.6 | The article was substantially compressed and clarified: the Electoral Commission and Accountability Commission now have cleaner mandate, powers, limits, quorum, vacancy, and continuity rules without the prior mini code structure | The next question is not architecture but edge case validation, especially internal anti sabotage and continuity scenarios | | 2026 03 29 | Article IV / Article XVI / Article XIX / Overall | 8.0 / 8.5 / 8.5 / 8.9 | 8.5 / 8.8 / 8.8 / 8.9 | The latest simulation reruns validated three important containment chains: expedited case Supreme Court delay now ends in party level finality, the war powers chain now clearly executes through fiscal hold and automatic impeachment transmission, and Article XIX transition deadlines now convert into continuity preserving fallback rather than open paralysis | The dominant remaining weakness is recurring legislative deadline failure, with smaller judicial delay and institutional deadline stress points still live | | 2026 03 28 | Preamble | 9.0 | 9.0 | Design notes were extracted from the operative preamble into a separate drafting document, leaving the preamble file as pure constitutional text without changing its substance | Remaining work is still limited to cadence and memorability | | 2026 03 29 | Articles XIV / XV / Overall | 8.0 / 8.5 / 8.6 | 8.3 / 8.8 / 8.7 | The fiscal de duplication pass moved the substantive continuity, debt, appropriations, and classified spending rules out of Article II and into the dedicated revenue and budget articles, making the fiscal architecture much clearer | The fiscal articles now need edge case testing and a later compression pass so stronger safeguards do not re accumulate as code like detail | | 2026 03 29 | Article IX / Overall | 8.0 / 8.7 | 8.4 / 8.8 | The citizenship de duplication pass made Article IX the clearer home of equal citizenship, overseas political membership, and general officeholding equality, while Articles I and III now read more cleanly as application articles | The remaining work is to tighten loss of citizenship standards and add targeted citizenship and eligibility stress tests | | 2026 03 29 | Article XI / Article XIX / Overall | 8.5 / 8.0 / 8.8 | 8.6 / 8.5 / 8.9 | The transition de duplication pass made Article XIX the clearer home of ratification, first cycle implementation, and transition law, while Articles III, IV, and XI now read more cleanly as branch specific or permanent rule articles | The remaining work is to pressure test first cycle failure scenarios and compress any residual implementation detail that still reads more like transition code than constitutional principle | | 2026 03 28 | Articles IX / XIV / XV / XIX / Overall | — | 8.0 / 8.0 / 8.5 / 8.0 / 8.6 | The new citizenship, taxation, budget, and transition articles were filled out into actual draft articles and now deserve direct scoring rather than being treated as structural placeholders | The new articles still need integration cleanup with adjacent operative articles and have not yet had full simulation or migration pressure testing | | 2026 03 28 | Article VIII / Article IX / Article X / Cross References | 8.5 / 8.5 / 8.5 / 8.0 | 8.5 / 8.5 / 8.5 / 8.5 | The amendment transition placeholder was closed, Constitutional Organs vacancy rules were made more coherent, the Article X sunset contradiction was resolved, and live cross reference drift was reduced further | The main remaining work is simplification and stress testing rather than contradiction repair | | 2026 03 28 | Preamble | 9.0 | 9.0 | The anti capture clause now ties democratic self defense more directly to law and equal citizenship, reducing the risk that the line could be misread as a free floating warrant for partisan suppression | Remaining work is still cadence and memorability, not substance | | 2026 03 28 | Preamble | 9.0 | 9.0 | Conservative preamble refinement replaced the slightly more abstract "world capable of sustaining life" with the more concrete "planet capable of sustaining life" and confirmed no meaningful exploit or mismatch with the operative articles | Remaining work is limited to final cadence polish rather than substantive revision | | 2026 03 28 | Preamble | 8.5 | 9.0 | The preamble was rewritten into tighter, more declarative founding prose while preserving its commitments to equal inclusion, accountable government, future generations, and democratic self defense | One more light polish pass could still improve cadence and memorability | | 2026 03 28 | Article III — Executive | 8.5 | 9.0 | Emergency powers, incapacity, recall, and presidential accountability rules were simplified into cleaner constitutional triggers with less procedural clutter | Recall and crisis scenarios should still be stress tested against edge case abuse | | 2026 03 28 | Article II / Article III / Cross References | 9.0 / 9.0 / 8.5 | 9.0 / 9.0 / 9.0 | Mandatory vote continuity rules, emergency lapse hardening, judicial fallback integration, and the new recess evasion scenario closed a real text to simulation gap and made cross article enforcement fit together more cleanly | Legislative nonperformance is still the main practical stress point even though the constitutional machinery is now clearer | | 2026 03 28 | Scorecard recalibration — Art II / III / IX / XI / XII / Cross Refs / Overall | 9.0 / 9.0 / 8.5 / 9.0 / 9.0 / 9.0 / 9.0 | 8.5 / 8.5 / 8.0 / 8.5 / 8.5 / 8.5 / 8.5 | Scores were inflated relative to open work: §15A(e)/(f) untested in simulation; Art XII draft discipline (7.0) inconsistent with 9.0 overall; Art IX has two open High priority tasks; Art XI enforcement unvalidated under adversarial command conditions; Cross References bumped prematurely before recess evasion scenario existed | Recess evasion simulation gap; XII compression; XI adversarial stress testing | | 2026 03 28 | Art II / Art III / Cross References | 8.5 / 8.5 / 8.5 | 9.0 / 9.0 / 9.0 | Recess evasion scenario (ra recess evasion.json) built and verified — §15A.1(e)/(f) and §5.4 recess evasion bar produce correct end to end enforcement chain with 0 unresolved obligations; enforceability scores restored to 9.0 | Remaining work for II/III is drafting discipline and re declaration/lapse certificate edge cases; XI adversarial stress testing is next priority | | 2026 03 28 | Article XI / Article XV | 8.5 / 8.5 | 9.0 / 9.0 | Article XI keeps its safeguards with cleaner doctrine level rules, and Article XV now states stronger constitutional floors with less code like implementation detail | Remaining work is mostly polishing and a final global consistency sweep rather than structural redesign | | 2026 03 28 | Article VII / Article XIV | 8.0 / 8.0 | 8.5 / 8.5 | Democratic floor enforcement was simplified, and the non war national security article now has a sharper boundary with Article XI | Article XI still has some operationally dense provisions that could be compressed further in a later pass | | 2026 03 28 | Article V / Article X / Cross References | 8.0 / 8.0 / 7.0 | 8.5 / 8.5 / 8.0 | Article V and X were trimmed to more constitutional abstraction levels, and stale drafting log drift was corrected | Federalism and war/foreign policy boundary rules still need later simplification and audit work | | 2026 03 28 | Article IV / Article XIII | 7.5 / 7.5 | 8.0 / 8.0 | Article IV appointment and governance machinery simplified; Article XIII remedies narrowed to adequacy review with clearer institutional limits | Full cross reference audit and later simplification passes still needed in Articles V, X, and VII | | 2026 03 28 | Initial baseline | — | 8.4 overall | First structured scorecard created | Continue simplification and consistency review | Review Checklist Before raising a score, ask: Did the revision make the text clearer? Did it reduce contradiction risk? Did it improve enforceability in practice? Did it preserve democratic legitimacy? Did it remove detail that belongs in statute instead? Did it avoid introducing new cross reference drift? Current Weaknesses The draft is now in refinement mode rather than redesign mode. The main weaknesses are: 1. Simulation coverage is now broad rather than selective. Remaining scenario work is mostly optional refinement, not a major article level validation deficit. 2. Residual code like density. Article II remains the densest core article, and parts of Articles VIII, XIII, and XIX still need a later compression pass. 3. Political nonperformance under stress. The Constitution now usually provides an automatic consequence, but repeated missed deadlines and refusals to act remain the dominant operational stress pattern in the simulator. 4. New resilience provisions that may later need compression. The recent anti oligarchic hardening improved abuse resistance materially, but some clauses should eventually be checked for constitutional abstraction level. 5. Final cross article boundary review. The remaining integration risk is not broad contradiction but narrower overlap at the boundaries of emergency power, ethics and agency design, continuity, and enforcement. Next Review Targets Recommended focus order: 1. Final cross article sweep — emergency overlaps, continuity language, stale references, and terminology consistency. 2. Article II — continue draft discipline cleanup only if another safe compression pass is obvious. 3. Article I / Article VI — add more soft exclusion work only if the quasi private boundary still feels constitutionally uncertain. 4. Article IX — add one later scenario on renunciation, statelessness avoidance, or foreign office incompatibility only if a real boundary question remains. 5. Article IV / Article XI — continue compression only where it clearly improves discipline without reducing enforceability. 6. Article XV / Article XVII — add later fiscal edge or gray zone scenarios only if a real boundary question remains. 7. Publication and explainer work — the draft is now in near final territory and more likely to benefit from reader facing clarification than from new machinery.
